common_name: Corn Cockle (Old-Fashioned Rose-Purple Cool-Season Annual from Grain Fields)

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (zone 9: September–November) or early spring where winters are cold; Corn Cockle does not transplant well and is best sown directly where it will grow. In zone 9, fall sowing produces the most robust plants and best flowering (February–April). The plant self-seeds reliably once established and will return each year from dropped seed without further planting. Seeds and all plant parts are toxic if ingested — do not plant where children or animals may eat them. The common name reflects the plant's original habitat as a weed of European cereal (corn) fields, where the purple flowers appeared among wheat and barley.

directions: Scatter seeds on prepared soil in fall or early spring; press in without covering deeply. Corn Cockle does not transplant well — sow in situ. Do not ingest seeds or plant material. Corn Cockle (Agrostemma githago) is a strikingly beautiful old-fashioned annual flower from the pink family (Caryophyllaceae) — a naturalized European wildflower (native to the Mediterranean region) that was once common in grain fields across Europe and Britain as a weed among cereal crops, where the large rose-purple flowers nodding on tall, slender, silvery-hairy stems created a dramatic and romantic visual effect in the spring grain fields. The flowers are 2–3 inches across, with five rounded petals in rose-purple to deep pink with a pale throat and distinctive darker veining and a ring of small dark spots at the base of each petal — a pattern of elegant complexity that makes individual flowers as beautiful when examined closely as the whole plant is from a distance. The long, narrow, silvery-hairy sepals that extend beyond the petals give the unopened buds a distinctive appearance and add texture to flower arrangements. Corn Cockle was once widespread as a grain field weed in Britain and Europe but has declined drastically due to herbicide use and improved grain seed cleaning, making it now more common as a cultivated garden plant than as a wild species — a poignant reversal for a once-ubiquitous wildflower. The seeds are poisonous (containing saponins called agrostemmic acid/githagin) — a significant historical concern when grain contaminated with corn cockle seeds was milled into flour, causing 'githagism' poisoning.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Agrostemma githago corn cockle variety notes
Royal Horticultural Society — Traditional British wildflower garden annuals
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Old-fashioned annual flower collection

common_name: Bishop of Llandaff Dahlia (Dark Bronze-Foliage Scarlet-Flowered Peony Dahlia Classic)

planting.note: Plant tubers horizontally 4–6 inches deep after last frost; Bishop of Llandaff is a peony-flowered dahlia type producing single to semi-double flowers with open centers. In zone 9, plant March–April for summer-fall bloom (July–November). The near-black to very dark bronze-purple foliage is the defining characteristic — this dark coloration is most intense in full sun. Bishop of Llandaff can be overwintered in zone 9 by leaving tubers in the ground with a layer of mulch for frost protection, or by digging tubers after frost kills the tops and storing in a cool, dry location (40–50°F). The contrast between the dark foliage and bright scarlet flowers makes this one of the most visually dramatic garden plants available.

directions: Plant tuber horizontally 4–6 inches deep with the growth eye facing up. Pinch the growing tip when 12 inches tall to encourage branching. Stake plants before they need it. Bishop of Llandaff is a dahlia cultivar of exceptional distinction — one of the small number of dahlia varieties that has achieved genuine iconic status, recognized by name even by gardeners with limited interest in dahlias, for the extraordinary combination of near-black bronze-purple foliage and vivid scarlet-red peony-form flowers. Most dahlias produce green foliage; Bishop of Llandaff's foliage is so deeply pigmented with anthocyanins that it appears nearly black in strong sunlight — a dramatic dark background that makes the bright red flowers appear to glow by contrast. The name honors the Bishop of Llandaff Cathedral in Cardiff, Wales; the variety was hybridized in 1928 by Mr. F. Hyde and has maintained its position as one of the most distinctive and recognizable dahlia varieties across nearly a century of cultivation. The peony-form flowers (semi-double with a visible open center) are 3–4 inches across, a clear, vivid scarlet-red — not the orange-red of some red dahlias but a pure, saturated scarlet — with a yellow-centered disc that provides important pollinator access. Unlike many fully double dahlia types, Bishop of Llandaff's open centers are accessible to bees and other pollinators, making it more ecologically valuable than many showier garden dahlias. It remains one of the most sought-after dahlias in British and European cottage garden traditions.
sources: American Dahlia Society — Bishop of Llandaff dahlia classification and history
Royal Horticultural Society — Bishop of Llandaff as AGM-winning dahlia
Brent and Becky's Bulbs — Bishop of Llandaff dahlia variety notes

common_name: Café au Lait Dahlia (Giant Peach-Cream Decorative Dahlia, Most Photographed Variety)

planting.note: Plant tubers horizontally 4–6 inches deep after last frost; Café au Lait is a formal decorative dahlia type producing very large (8–12 inch) blooms on tall (4–5 foot) plants. Stake plants early — the heavy blooms on long stems require support. In zone 9, plant March–April for summer-fall bloom; the warm Gulf Coast summers can cause some bloom reduction in peak July–August heat, with best flowering in June and September–November. Pinch the growing tip when 12–18 inches tall to encourage multiple blooms per plant. The peach-cream-blush coloring is most defined in cool fall weather; summer heat can cause the coloring to wash out slightly to a paler cream.

directions: Plant tubers horizontally 4–6 inches deep with the growth eye facing up; stake immediately. Pinch growing tip when 12 inches tall. Café au Lait is arguably the most widely photographed and socially media-documented dahlia variety of the modern era — a formal decorative dahlia with dinner-plate-sized blooms (8–12 inches diameter) in the distinctive warm blush-peach-cream-coffee coloring that gives the variety its French café name. The color is not easily described in conventional color terms: it is a complex, warm-toned mixture of cream, peachy-buff, blush pink, and very light coffee-tan that shifts perceptibly across the bloom from center to edge and changes character in different lighting — appearing more coral-peachy in warm golden afternoon light and more cream-pink in cooler morning light. This complex, warm-neutral coloring has made Café au Lait the most popular dahlia in the wedding flower industry and in the aesthetic sensibility of social-media garden photography, where its warm, muted tones photograph beautifully and complement the neutral color palettes favored in contemporary interior and event design. As a garden plant, Café au Lait is physically impressive — the enormous blooms on 4–5 foot plants are genuinely spectacular from late summer through fall — with the added utility of being one of the most spectacular and long-lasting cut flowers available, vase life extending 7–10 days when cut in the morning at the proper maturity stage.
sources: American Dahlia Society — Café au Lait formal decorative classification
Swan Island Dahlias — Café au Lait variety notes and commercial production
Floret Flower Farm — Café au Lait as premier cut-flower dahlia

common_name: Dr. Wyche's Yellow Tomato (Oklahoma Heirloom Large Orange-Yellow Beefsteak Tomato)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Dr. Wyche's Yellow is an indeterminate beefsteak type requiring staking or caging. In zone 9, transplant February–March for spring production; the tomato's excellent flavor quality at full orange-yellow ripeness makes timely harvest important — overripe fruits crack and lose flavor. The color at full ripeness is a warm orange-gold to orange-yellow, not pale yellow or greenish — harvest when the fruit has achieved a uniform orange-gold color and has slight give to gentle pressure.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–80°F; transplant after last frost. Stake or cage. Harvest when uniformly orange-gold and slightly soft. Dr. Wyche's Yellow is a large-fruited heirloom tomato from an extraordinary source: Dr. John Wyche, a circus man from Hugo, Oklahoma, who operated King Brothers Circus and simultaneously maintained an extensive collection of heirloom seeds that he distributed to seed savers and gardeners over many years. Dr. Wyche reportedly used manure from the circus animals (elephants, big cats, and other exotic animals) to fertilize his prodigious garden at his Hugo farm, giving his tomatoes — which he claimed grew unusually large and vigorous — the colorful story of being 'circus-fertilized.' The tomatoes that bear his name are large beefsteak-type fruits ripening to a warm orange-gold to orange-yellow, with a complex, rich flavor that many tasters describe as fruity, sweet, and low-acid with more depth than typical yellow tomatoes. The fruits are large (often 12–16 oz or more under ideal conditions) and meaty — the solid orange-yellow flesh is dense and flavorful with relatively few seed cavities for a beefsteak type. Dr. Wyche's Yellow has been distributed by Seed Savers Exchange and other heirloom seed sources and has gained a devoted following among heirloom tomato enthusiasts who value the combination of large size, distinctive warm orange-gold color, and excellent flavor.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Dr. Wyche's Yellow tomato heirloom documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Dr. Wyche's Yellow variety notes
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Large-fruited yellow tomato variety comparison

common_name: Super Fantastic Tomato (Burpee F1 Hybrid Large Vigorous Disease-Resistant Slicer)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Super Fantastic is indeterminate and requires staking or caging — the plant produces heavy crops of large fruits over a long season that weigh down unsupported canes. In zone 9, transplant February–March for spring production. Super Fantastic is noted for producing fruits later into the warm Gulf Coast spring than some other beefsteak varieties — its heat tolerance gives it slightly extended production in zone 9 before summer heat stops fruit set. The disease resistance (VF — Verticillium and Fusarium wilt resistance) is particularly valuable in the Gulf South's humid soil conditions.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 75–80°F; transplant after last frost. Stake or cage immediately. Super Fantastic is a Burpee F1 hybrid tomato released in 1978 — one of the most successful and widely sold hybrid tomatoes of the latter 20th century, bred at the W. Atlee Burpee Company by Dr. Elwyn Meader's breeding program. Super Fantastic hit the market at a time when home gardeners were increasingly adopting the first generation of disease-resistant hybrid tomatoes, and the combination of large fruit size (fruits commonly 12–16 oz), vigorous plant growth, early maturity (70–80 days), and dual disease resistance (VF designation) made it extremely popular in the American home garden market through the 1980s and 1990s. The fruits are classic large red beefsteak form — round to slightly oblate, firm, meaty, with relatively few seeds and good thick walls — the standard large slicing tomato character for sandwiches, burgers, and fresh eating. Super Fantastic remains in production and widely sold despite the explosion of alternative hybrid and heirloom beefsteak types since its introduction, maintained in home garden seed racks and catalogs by its reputation for reliable, heavy production and disease resistance across a wide range of conditions. For zone 9 gardeners, the VF disease resistance tag is particularly relevant in humid soils where Fusarium and Verticillium wilt are common causes of premature plant death.
sources: W. Atlee Burpee Company — Super Fantastic tomato F1 hybrid release notes 1978
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Large hybrid beefsteak tomato variety comparison
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Disease-Resistant Tomato Variety Recommendations
